21.notes:
Joshua - Chapter 21
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The Levites receive forty-eight cities scattered throughout all the tribes, since the LORD Himself is their inheritance. The chapter concludes: not one word of God's promises has failed—all came to pass.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- 48 Levitical cities distributed throughout all tribal territories — Levites had no single tribal allotment
- Kohathites received cities in Judah, Simeon, Benjamin, Ephraim, Dan, and west Manasseh
- Gershonites received cities in Issachar, Asher, Naphtali, and east Manasseh; Merarites in Zebulun, Reuben, and Gad

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Numbers     | 35      | 1-8                | God's command to give Levitical cities.
v.3              | Leviticus   | 25      | 32-34              | Levitical cities cannot be permanently sold; their pasturelands are perpetual.
v.11             | Joshua      | 14      | 13-14              | Hebron given to Caleb; here the city itself (minus fields/villages) is also a Levitical/priestly city.
v.12             | Joshua      | 14      | 13                 | Caleb's fields and villages distinguished from the city proper given to the Levites.
v.41             | Numbers     | 35      | 7                  | "All the cities which you shall give to the Levites shall be forty and eight cities."
v.43             | Genesis     | 15      | 18-21              | God's land promise to Abraham.
v.43             | Deuteronomy | 1       | 8                  | "Go in and possess the land which the LORD swore to your fathers."
v.44             | Deuteronomy | 12      | 10                 | "He gives you rest from all your enemies round about."
v.44             | Hebrews     | 4       | 8-9                | "If Joshua had given them rest, then would he not afterward have spoken of another day."
v.45             | Joshua      | 23      | 14                 | Joshua repeats this affirmation at his farewell.
v.45             | 1 Kings     | 8       | 56                 | Solomon echoes: "There has not failed one word of all his good promise."
v.45             | Numbers     | 23      | 19                 | "God is not a man, that he should lie... has he said, and shall he not do it?"

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/joshua-21/
  Guzik emphasizes vv.43-45 as the theological heart of the book of
  Joshua. God's faithfulness is absolute — not one promise failed. He
  notes that this does not mean every individual Canaanite was expelled
  (chapters 15-17 show otherwise), but that God fulfilled His covenant
  obligations. Israel's incomplete possession was their failure, not
  God's. The promise was kept; the appropriation was partial.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'There failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken.'
  Not one thing! Can you find a single promise of God that has fallen to
  the ground? Search the Scriptures — has He ever failed you? Has He
  ever spoken and not done it? Let this verse be your anchor: ALL came
  to pass. What He has promised for your future, He will perform with
  the same perfection. Not one word shall fail."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "suburbs thereof for our cattle" / ESV: "pasturelands for our livestock" — Hebrew "migrash" = open land, pasture ground surrounding a city. As noted in 14:4, KJV's "suburbs" is misleading to modern readers. ESV's "pasturelands" correctly identifies these as grazing areas, not residential neighborhoods.
v.11             | KJV         | "the city of Arba the father of Anak" / ESV: "Kiriath-arba (Arba being the father of Anak)" — Note that Hebron is simultaneously Caleb's inheritance (14:13), a city of refuge (20:7), and a Levitical city (21:13). The fields and villages belonged to Caleb (v.12); the city itself served multiple sacred functions.
v.43             | KJV         | "all the land which he sware to give unto their fathers" / ESV: "all the land that he swore to give to their fathers" — Hebrew "nishba" = swore (niphal of "shava"). God's promise was not merely spoken but sworn — an oath. The strongest possible commitment (cf. Hebrews 6:13-18).
v.44             | KJV         | "there stood not a man of all their enemies before them" / ESV: "Not one of all their enemies had withstood them" — Hebrew "lo amad ish... mipneihem." Literally "no man stood before them." This echoes God's promise to Joshua in 1:5: "There shall not any man be able to stand before thee." Promise made — promise kept.
v.45             | KJV         | "There failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken" / ESV: "Not one word of all the good promises that the LORD had made... had failed" — Hebrew "lo naphal davar mi-kol ha-davar ha-tov." "Naphal" = to fall. Not one word "fell" — i.e., fell to the ground unfulfilled. Every word stood firm. ESV adds "word" and "promises" for clarity; KJV's "ought" (anything) is more literal but archaic.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Levitical Cities  | 48 cities distributed among the three Levitical clans (Kohathites, Gershonites, Merarites) throughout all tribal territories. Included the 6 cities of refuge. Provided dwelling places and pasturelands for Levites who had no territorial inheritance. | 
Kohathites        | Descendants of Kohath, Levi's second son. Divided into priestly (Aaron's line) and non-priestly branches. The priests received cities in Judah, Simeon, and Benjamin (near the future temple site). Other Kohathites received cities in Ephraim, Dan, and Manasseh. | 
Gershonites       | Descendants of Gershon (or Gershom), Levi's firstborn. Received 13 cities in the northern tribes (Issachar, Asher, Naphtali) and eastern half-Manasseh. | 
Merarites         | Descendants of Merari, Levi's third son. Received 12 cities in Zebulun, Reuben, and Gad — the peripheral territories. | 
Pasturelands (migrash) | Open land surrounding Levitical cities, extending 1,000 cubits from the city wall in each direction (Numbers 35:4-5). Used for grazing livestock. Could not be sold (Leviticus 25:34). | 
Rest (menukhah)   | Hebrew concept of peace, security, and settled dwelling. The goal of the conquest was not merely land but rest — freedom from enemies, security in God's presence. Ultimately points to the eternal rest in Christ (Hebrews 4:9-11). | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "There failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken; all
   came to pass" (v.45). This is the Bible's own testimony about God's
   faithfulness. Not one promise failed. Not one word fell. What God says,
   He does. What He promises, He performs. This is the foundation of all
   faith: God keeps His word — perfectly, completely, without exception.

2. Levites scattered as teachers (v.41). What began as a curse (Genesis
   49:7) became a blessing. The Levites' scattering meant every tribe had
   access to God's teachers. God transforms our curses into blessings. What
   seems like judgment may become our greatest ministry.

3. Shared responsibility (v.3). Every tribe gave cities to the Levites.
   Supporting those in full-time ministry is not optional — it is
   commanded. The body of Christ supports its teachers, pastors, and
   missionaries as Israel supported its Levites.

4. God gives rest (v.44). The conquest's goal was not merely territory but
   rest — peace on every side. God's ultimate gift to His people is rest
   (Matthew 11:28; Hebrews 4:9). Have you entered His rest? Or are you
   still striving in your own strength?

5. Promise and possession (v.43). God gave the land AND they possessed it
   AND they dwelt in it. Three stages: God's gift, human appropriation,
   settled enjoyment. God gives — we must receive and inhabit. Salvation is
   a gift, but we must live in it daily.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Joshua Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/joshua/

David Guzik — Joshua 21 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/joshua-21/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

22.ESV:
1. At that time Joshua summoned the Reubenites and the Gadites and the half-tribe of Manasseh,
2. and said to them, “You have kept all that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you and have obeyed my voice in all that I have commanded you.
3. You have not forsaken your brothers these many days, down to this day, but have been careful to keep the charge of the LORD your God.
4. And now the LORD your God has given rest to your brothers, as he promised them. Therefore turn and go to your tents in the land where your possession lies, which Moses the servant of the LORD gave you on the other side of the Jordan.
5. Only be very careful to observe the commandment and the law that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you, to love the LORD your God, and to walk in all his ways and to keep his commandments and to cling to him and to serve him with all your heart and with all your soul.”
6. So Joshua blessed them and sent them away, and they went to their tents.
7. Now to the one half of the tribe of Manasseh Moses had given a possession in Bashan, but to the other half Joshua had given a possession beside their brothers in the land west of the Jordan. And when Joshua sent them away to their homes and blessed them,
8. he said to them, “Go back to your tents with much wealth and with very much livestock, with silver, gold, bronze, and iron, and with much clothing. Divide the spoil of your enemies with your brothers.”
9. So the people of Reuben and the people of Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh returned home, parting from the people of Israel at Shiloh, which is in the land of Canaan, to go to the land of Gilead, their own land of which they had possessed themselves by command of the LORD through Moses.
10. And when they came to the region of the Jordan that is in the land of Canaan, the people of Reuben and the people of Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh built there an altar by the Jordan, an altar of imposing size.
11. And the people of Israel heard it said, “Behold, the people of Reuben and the people of Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh have built the altar at the frontier of the land of Canaan, in the region about the Jordan, on the side that belongs to the people of Israel.”
12. And when the people of Israel heard of it, the whole assembly of the people of Israel gathered at Shiloh to make war against them.
13. Then the people of Israel sent to the people of Reuben and the people of Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh, in the land of Gilead, Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest,
14. and with him ten chiefs, one from each of the tribal families of Israel, every one of them the head of a family among the clans of Israel.
15. And they came to the people of Reuben, the people of Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, in the land of Gilead, and they said to them,
16. “Thus says the whole congregation of the LORD, ‘What is this breach of faith that you have committed against the God of Israel in turning away this day from following the LORD by building yourselves an altar this day in rebellion against the LORD?
17. Have we not had enough of the sin at Peor from which even yet we have not cleansed ourselves, and for which there came a plague upon the congregation of the LORD,
18. that you too must turn away this day from following the LORD? And if you too rebel against the LORD today then tomorrow he will be angry with the whole congregation of Israel.
19. But now, if the land of your possession is unclean, pass over into the LORD’s land where the LORD’s tabernacle stands, and take for yourselves a possession among us. Only do not rebel against the LORD or make us as rebels by building for yourselves an altar other than the altar of the LORD our God.
20. Did not Achan the son of Zerah break faith in the matter of the devoted things, and wrath fell upon all the congregation of Israel? And he did not perish alone for his iniquity.’”
21. Then the people of Reuben, the people of Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh said in answer to the heads of the families of Israel,
22. “The Mighty One, God, the LORD! The Mighty One, God, the LORD! He knows; and let Israel itself know! If it was in rebellion or in breach of faith against the LORD, do not spare us today
23. for building an altar to turn away from following the LORD. Or if we did so to offer burnt offerings or grain offerings or peace offerings on it, may the LORD himself take vengeance.
24. No, but we did it from fear that in time to come your children might say to our children, ‘What have you to do with the LORD, the God of Israel?
25. For the LORD has made the Jordan a boundary between us and you, you people of Reuben and people of Gad. You have no portion in the LORD.’ So your children might make our children cease to worship the LORD.
26. Therefore we said, ‘Let us now build an altar, not for burnt offering, nor for sacrifice,
27. but to be a witness between us and you, and between our generations after us, that we do perform the service of the LORD in his presence with our burnt offerings and sacrifices and peace offerings, so your children will not say to our children in time to come, “You have no portion in the LORD.”’
28. And we thought, ‘If this should be said to us or to our descendants in time to come, we should say, “Behold, the copy of the altar of the LORD, which our fathers made, not for burnt offerings, nor for sacrifice, but to be a witness between us and you.”’
29. Far be it from us that we should rebel against the LORD and turn away this day from following the LORD by building an altar for burnt offering, grain offering, or sacrifice, other than the altar of the LORD our God that stands before his tabernacle!”
30. When Phinehas the priest and the chiefs of the congregation, the heads of the families of Israel who were with him, heard the words that the people of Reuben and the people of Gad and the people of Manasseh spoke, it was good in their eyes.
31. And Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest said to the people of Reuben and the people of Gad and the people of Manasseh, “Today we know that the LORD is in our midst, because you have not committed this breach of faith against the LORD. Now you have delivered the people of Israel from the hand of the LORD.”
32. Then Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest, and the chiefs, returned from the people of Reuben and the people of Gad in the land of Gilead to the land of Canaan, to the people of Israel, and brought back word to them.
33. And the report was good in the eyes of the people of Israel. And the people of Israel blessed God and spoke no more of making war against them to destroy the land where the people of Reuben and the people of Gad were settled.
34. The people of Reuben and the people of Gad called the altar Witness, “For,” they said, “it is a witness between us that the LORD is God.”
22.KJV:
1. Then Joshua called the Reubenites, and the Gadites, and the half tribe of Manasseh,
2. And said unto them, Ye have kept all that Moses the servant of the LORD commanded you, and have obeyed my voice in all that I commanded you:
3. Ye have not left your brethren these many days unto this day, but have kept the charge of the commandment of the LORD your God.
4. And now the LORD your God hath given rest unto your brethren, as he promised them: therefore now return ye, and get you unto your tents, and unto the land of your possession, which Moses the servant of the LORD gave you on the other side Jordan.
5. But take diligent heed to do the commandment and the law, which Moses the servant of the LORD charged you, to love the LORD your God, and to walk in all his ways, and to keep his commandments, and to cleave unto him, and to serve him with all your heart and with all your soul.
6. So Joshua blessed them, and sent them away: and they went unto their tents.

And the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh returned, and departed from the children of Israel out of Shiloh, which is in the land of Canaan, to go unto the country of Gilead, to the land of their possession, whereof they were possessed, according to the word of the LORD by the hand of Moses.
10. And when they came unto the borders of Jordan, that are in the land of Canaan, the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh built there an altar by Jordan, a great altar to see to.

14. And with him ten princes, of each chief house a prince throughout all the tribes of Israel; and each one was an head of the house of their fathers among the thousands of Israel.
15. And they came unto the children of Reuben, and to the children of Gad, and to the half tribe of Manasseh, unto the land of Gilead, and they spake with them, saying,

17. Is the iniquity of Peor too little for us, from which we are not cleansed until this day, although there was a plague in the congregation of the LORD,
18. But that ye must turn away this day from following the LORD? and it will be, seeing ye rebel to day against the LORD, that to morrow he will be wroth with the whole congregation of Israel.
19. Notwithstanding, if the land of your possession be unclean, then pass ye over unto the land of the possession of the LORD, wherein the LORD’S tabernacle dwelleth, and take possession among us: but rebel not against the LORD, nor rebel against us, in building you an altar beside the altar of the LORD our God.
20. Did not Achan the son of Zerah commit a trespass in the accursed thing, and wrath fell on all the congregation of Israel? and that man perished not alone in his iniquity.
21. Then the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the half tribe of Manasseh answered, and said unto the heads of the thousands of Israel,
22. The LORD God of gods, the LORD God of gods, he knoweth, and Israel he shall know; if it be in rebellion, or if in transgression against the LORD, (save us not this day,)

24. And if we have not rather done it for fear of this thing, saying, In time to come your children might speak unto our children, saying, What have ye to do with the LORD God of Israel?
25. For the LORD hath made Jordan a border between us and you, ye children of Reuben and children of Gad; ye have no part in the LORD: so shall your children make our children cease from fearing the LORD.
26. Therefore we said, Let us now prepare to build us an altar, not for burnt offering, nor for sacrifice:
27. But that it may be a witness between us, and you, and our generations after us, that we might do the service of the LORD before him with our burnt offerings, and with our sacrifices, and with our peace offerings; that your children may not say to our children in time to come, Ye have no part in the LORD.
28. Therefore said we, that it shall be, when they should so say to us or to our generations in time to come, that we may say again, Behold the pattern of the altar of the LORD, which our fathers made, not for burnt offerings, nor for sacrifices; but it is a witness between us and you.
29. God forbid that we should rebel against the LORD, and turn this day from following the LORD, to build an altar for burnt offerings, for meat offerings, or for sacrifices, beside the altar of the LORD our God that is before his tabernacle.

33. And the thing pleased the children of Israel; and the children of Israel blessed God, and did not intend to go up against them in battle, to destroy the land wherein the children of Reuben and Gad dwelt.
34. And the children of Reuben and the children of Gad called the altar Ed: for it shall be a witness between us that the LORD is God.

22.NET:
1. Then Joshua summoned the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh
2. and told them: “You have carried out all the instructions of Moses the Lord’s servant, and you have obeyed all I have told you.
3. You have not abandoned your fellow Israelites this entire time, right up to this very day. You have completed the task given you by the Lord your God.
4. Now the Lord your God has made your fellow Israelites secure, just as he promised them. So now you may turn around and go to your homes in your own land that Moses the Lord’s servant assigned to you east of the Jordan.
5. But carefully obey the commands and instructions Moses the Lord’s servant gave you. Love the Lord your God, follow all his instructions, obey his commands, be loyal to him, and serve him with all your heart and being!”
6. Joshua rewarded them and sent them on their way; they returned to their homes.
7. (Now to one half-tribe of Manasseh, Moses had assigned land in Bashan; and to the other half Joshua had assigned land on the west side of the Jordan with their fellow Israelites.) When Joshua sent them home, he rewarded them,
8. saying, “Take home great wealth, a lot of cattle, silver, gold, bronze, iron, and a lot of clothing. Divide up the goods captured from your enemies with your brothers.”
9. So the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh left the Israelites in Shiloh in the land of Canaan and headed home to their own land in Gilead, which they acquired by the Lord’s command through Moses.
10. The Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh came to Geliloth near the Jordan in the land of Canaan and built there, near the Jordan, an impressive altar.
11. The Israelites received this report: “Look, the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh have built an altar at the entrance to the land of Canaan, at Geliloth near the Jordan on the Israelite side.”
12. When the Israelites heard this, the entire Israelite community assembled at Shiloh to launch an attack against them.
13. The Israelites sent Phinehas son of Eleazar, the priest, to the land of Gilead to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh.
14. He was accompanied by 10 leaders, one from each of the Israelite tribes, each one a family leader among the Israelite clans.
15. They went to the land of Gilead to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh, and said to them:
16. “The entire community of the Lord says, ‘Why have you disobeyed the God of Israel by turning back today from following the Lord? You built an altar for yourselves and have rebelled today against the Lord.
17. The sin we committed at Peor was bad enough. To this very day we have not purified ourselves; it even brought a plague on the community of the Lord.
18. Now today you dare to turn back from following the Lord! You are rebelling today against the Lord; tomorrow he may break out in anger against the entire community of Israel.
19. But if your own land is impure, cross over to the Lord’s own land, where the Lord himself lives, and settle down among us. But don’t rebel against the Lord or us by building for yourselves an altar other than the altar of the Lord our God.
20. When Achan son of Zerah disobeyed the command about the city’s riches, the entire Israelite community was judged, though only one man had sinned. He most certainly died for his sin!’”
21. The Reubenites, the Gadites, and the half-tribe of Manasseh answered the leaders of the Israelite clans:
22. “El, God, the Lord! El, God, the Lord! He knows the truth! Israel must also know! If we have rebelled or disobeyed the Lord, don’t spare us today!
23. If we have built an altar for ourselves to turn back from following the Lord by making burnt sacrifices and grain offerings on it, or by offering tokens of peace on it, the Lord himself will punish us.
24. We swear we have done this because we were worried that in the future your descendants would say to our descendants, ‘What relationship do you have with the Lord God of Israel?
25. The Lord made the Jordan a boundary between us and you Reubenites and Gadites. You have no right to worship the Lord.’ In this way your descendants might cause our descendants to stop obeying the Lord.
26. So we decided to build this altar, not for burnt offerings and sacrifices,
27. but as a reminder to us and you and our descendants who follow us, that we will honor the Lord in his very presence with burnt offerings, sacrifices, and tokens of peace. Then in the future your descendants will not be able to say to our descendants, ‘You have no right to worship the Lord.’
28. We said, ‘If in the future they say such a thing to us or to our descendants, we will reply, “See the model of the Lord’s altar that our ancestors made, not for burnt offerings or sacrifices, but as a reminder to us and you.”’
29. Far be it from us to rebel against the Lord by turning back today from following after the Lord by building an altar for burnt offerings, sacrifices, and tokens of peace aside from the altar of the Lord our God located in front of his dwelling place!”
30. When Phinehas the priest and the community leaders and Israel’s clan leaders who accompanied him heard the defense of the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the Manassehites, they were satisfied.
31. Phinehas son of Eleazar, the priest, said to the Reubenites, the Gadites, and the Manassehites, “Today we know that the Lord is among us because you have not disobeyed the Lord in this. Now you have rescued the Israelites from the Lord’s judgment.”
32. Phinehas son of Eleazar, the priest, and the leaders left the Reubenites and Gadites in the land of Gilead and reported back to the Israelites in the land of Canaan.
33. The Israelites were satisfied with their report and gave thanks to God. They said nothing more about launching an attack to destroy the land in which the Reubenites and Gadites lived.
34. The Reubenites and Gadites named the altar, “Surely it is a Reminder to us that the Lord is God.”

11. The children of Israel heard this, “Behold, the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh have built an altar in the border of the land of Canaan, in the region around the Jordan, on the side that belongs to the children of Israel.”
12. When the children of Israel heard of it, the whole congregation of the children of Israel gathered themselves together at Shiloh, to go up against them to war.
13. The children of Israel sent to the children of Reuben, and to the children of Gad, and to the half-tribe of Manasseh, into the land of Gilead, Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest.
14. With him were ten princes, one prince of a fathers’ house for each of the tribes of Israel; and they were each head of their fathers’ houses among the thousands of Israel.
15. They came to the children of Reuben, and to the children of Gad, and to the half-tribe of Manasseh, to the land of Gilead, and they spoke with them, saying,
16. “Thus says the whole congregation of Yahweh, ‘What trespass is this that you have committed against the God of Israel, to turn away today from following Yahweh, in that you have built yourselves an altar, to rebel today against Yahweh?
17. Is the iniquity of Peor too little for us, from which we have not cleansed ourselves to this day, although there came a plague on the congregation of Yahweh,
18. that you must turn away today from following Yahweh? It will be, since you rebel today against Yahweh, that tomorrow he will be angry with the whole congregation of Israel.
19. However, if the land of your possession is unclean, then pass over to the land of the possession of Yahweh, in which Yahweh’s tabernacle dwells, and take possession among us; but don’t rebel against Yahweh, nor rebel against us, in building an altar other than Yahweh our God’s altar.
20. Didn’t Achan the son of Zerah commit a trespass in the devoted thing, and wrath fell on all the congregation of Israel? That man didn’t perish alone in his iniquity.’”
21. Then the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the half-tribe of Manasseh answered, and spoke to the heads of the thousands of Israel,
22. “The Mighty One, God, Yahweh, the Mighty One, God, Yahweh, he knows; and Israel shall know: if it was in rebellion, or if in trespass against Yahweh (don’t save us today),
23. that we have built us an altar to turn away from following Yahweh; or if to offer burnt offering or meal offering, or if to offer sacrifices of peace offerings, let Yahweh himself require it.
24. “If we have not out of concern done this, and for a reason, saying, ‘In time to come your children might speak to our children, saying, “What have you to do with Yahweh, the God of Israel?
25. For Yahweh has made the Jordan a border between us and you, you children of Reuben and children of Gad. You have no portion in Yahweh.”’ So your children might make our children cease from fearing Yahweh.
26. “Therefore we said, ‘Let’s now prepare to build ourselves an altar, not for burnt offering, nor for sacrifice;
27. but it will be a witness between us and you, and between our generations after us, that we may perform the service of Yahweh before him with our burnt offerings, with our sacrifices, and with our peace offerings;’ that your children may not tell our children in time to come, ‘You have no portion in Yahweh.’
28. “Therefore we said, ‘It shall be, when they tell us or our generations this in time to come, that we shall say, “Behold the pattern of Yahweh’s altar, which our fathers made, not for burnt offering, nor for sacrifice; but it is a witness between us and you.”’
29. “Far be it from us that we should rebel against Yahweh, and turn away today from following Yahweh, to build an altar for burnt offering, for meal offering, or for sacrifice, besides Yahweh our God’s altar that is before his tabernacle!”
30. When Phinehas the priest, and the princes of the congregation, even the heads of the thousands of Israel that were with him, heard the words that the children of Reuben and the children of Gad and the children of Manasseh spoke, it pleased them well.
31. Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest said to the children of Reuben, to the children of Gad, and to the children of Manasseh, “Today we know that Yahweh is among us, because you have not committed this trespass against Yahweh. Now you have delivered the children of Israel out of Yahweh’s hand.”
32. Phinehas the son of Eleazar the priest, and the princes, returned from the children of Reuben, and from the children of Gad, out of the land of Gilead, to the land of Canaan, to the children of Israel, and brought them word again.
33. The thing pleased the children of Israel; and the children of Israel blessed God, and spoke no more of going up against them to war, to destroy the land in which the children of Reuben and the children of Gad lived.
34. The children of Reuben and the children of Gad named the altar “A Witness Between Us that Yahweh is God.”
22.notes:
Joshua - Chapter 22
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The Transjordan tribes build an altar by the Jordan, alarming the other tribes who fear apostasy. The near-civil war is averted when the eastern tribes explain it is a memorial of unity, not a rival altar.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- The Transjordan tribes (Reuben, Gad, half-Manasseh) return east across the Jordan to their allotments
- The great altar: Built on the east bank of the Jordan near the crossing point — visible from the western side
- The Jordan River serves as the boundary line that nearly divides the nation; the altar is meant to unify

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.2              | Numbers     | 32      | 20-22              | The original agreement: fight first, then return to your inheritance.
v.5              | Deuteronomy | 6       | 5                  | "You shall love the LORD your God with all your heart, and with all your soul, and with all your might."
v.5              | Deuteronomy | 10      | 12-13              | The same five-fold charge.
v.12             | Deuteronomy | 13      | 12-15              | Command to make war against apostate cities in Israel.
v.13             | Numbers     | 25      | 7-8                | Phinehas' zeal at Peor — he stopped the plague by executing the sinners.
v.17             | Numbers     | 25      | 1-9                | The sin at Peor: 24,000 died in the plague.
v.19             | Deuteronomy | 12      | 5-14               | Centralized worship at God's chosen place.
v.20             | Joshua      | 7       | 1-26               | Achan's sin and its corporate consequences.
v.22             | Deuteronomy | 10      | 17                 | "The LORD your God is God of gods, and Lord of lords."
v.25             | Deuteronomy | 12      | 8-9                | Warning against "every man doing what is right in his own eyes."
v.29             | Leviticus   | 17      | 8-9                | Prohibition of sacrifice outside the tabernacle.
v.34             | Genesis     | 31      | 48-52              | Jacob and Laban's witness heap (Galeed/ Mizpah) — a similar memorial of covenant between parties.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/joshua-22/
  Guzik highlights the wisdom of sending a delegation before going to
  war. The western tribes were right to be concerned about apostasy, but
  they were wise to investigate before attacking. He draws the
  application: in the church, we must confront potential sin — but we
  must also listen before condemning. Many conflicts arise from
  misunderstanding, not malice.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "How many wars — in churches, in families, in nations — could be
  prevented by a simple conversation! The western tribes assumed the
  worst. They were ready to fight. But they sent Phinehas first — and
  discovered that what looked like rebellion was actually devotion. Let
  us learn: before you draw the sword, send the messenger. Before you
  condemn, ask. Before you judge, listen. Many an 'altar of rebellion'
  is actually an altar of witness."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.5              | KJV         | "cleave unto him" / ESV: "cling to him" — Hebrew "u-l'davqah bo." "Davaq" = to cling, stick to, adhere. The same word used in Genesis 2:24 — a man shall "cleave" to his wife. It implies intimate, inseparable attachment. Our relationship to God should be as close as marriage.
v.10             | KJV         | "a great altar to see to" / ESV: "an altar of imposing size" — Hebrew "mizbeach gadol l'mar'eh." Literally "a great altar to appearance/sight." KJV's "to see to" is archaic but captures the visual impact. ESV's "imposing size" is clearer. The altar was deliberately conspicuous — meant to be seen from a distance.
v.16             | KJV         | "What trespass is this" / ESV: "What is this breach of faith" — Hebrew "mah ha-ma'al ha-zeh." "Ma'al" = unfaithfulness, treachery, breach of trust. A strong term implying betrayal of covenant relationship. ESV's "breach of faith" captures the relational dimension better than KJV's "trespass."
v.17             | KJV         | "Is the iniquity of Peor too little for us" / ESV: "Have we not had enough of the sin at Peor" — Hebrew "ha-m'at lanu et avon P'or." The delegation invokes Israel's most recent catastrophic sin — the idolatry and immorality at Baal-peor (Numbers 25) where 24,000 died. The memory is still fresh.
v.22             | KJV         | "The LORD God of gods, the LORD God of gods" / ESV: "The Mighty One, God, the LORD! The Mighty One, God, the LORD!" — Hebrew "El Elohim YHWH, El Elohim YHWH." Three divine names repeated twice — the most solemn oath formula possible. KJV renders all three as aspects of "LORD God"; ESV distinguishes them: "Mighty One" (El), "God" (Elohim), "the LORD" (YHWH).
v.28             | KJV         | "the pattern of the altar of the LORD" / ESV: "the copy of the altar of the LORD" — Hebrew "tavnit mizbach YHWH." "Tavnit" = pattern, model, copy, likeness. The altar was a replica — not a functioning altar but a representation pointing back to the true altar at the tabernacle.
v.34             | KJV         | "called the altar Ed" / ESV: "called the altar Witness" — Hebrew "Ed" = witness, testimony. KJV transliterates the Hebrew name; ESV translates it. The altar's name encapsulates its purpose: it testifies that the eastern tribes belong to the LORD.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Phinehas          | Son of Eleazar, grandson of Aaron. Known for his zeal at Peor where he executed an Israelite man and Midianite woman engaged in idolatrous immorality, stopping the plague (Numbers 25:7-13). God gave him a "covenant of peace" and perpetual priesthood. His selection to lead this delegation was strategic — he was zealous for God's honor but also a man of discernment. | 
Ed (Witness)      | The name given to the altar built by the eastern tribes. Hebrew "ed" = witness, testimony. The altar testified to future generations that the Transjordan tribes were part of Israel and worshiped the LORD. | 
Peor              | Baal-peor, the Moabite deity. At Shittim, Israel was seduced into idolatry and sexual immorality with Moabite women (Numbers 25). 24,000 died in the resulting plague. The delegation cites this as a warning of corporate consequences for apostasy. | 
Achan             | Son of Zerah, of the tribe of Judah. He took devoted things from Jericho (Joshua 7), bringing defeat at Ai and judgment on all Israel. Cited here (v.20) as proof that one person's sin affects the entire community. | 
Cleave/Cling (davaq) | To adhere, stick to, hold fast. Used of marriage (Genesis 2:24) and of devotion to God (Deuteronomy 10:20; 11:22). Implies intimate, unbreakable attachment. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. Communication prevents catastrophe (vv.13-14, 30-33). The western tribes
   were ready for war — but they sent a delegation first. That conversation
   saved Israel from civil war. How many conflicts in churches, families,
   and friendships could be resolved by simply asking before assuming? "Be
   swift to hear, slow to speak, slow to wrath" (James 1:19).

2. Zeal must be paired with wisdom (vv.12-14). The western tribes' zeal for
   God's honor was commendable — they took apostasy seriously. But zeal
   without investigation leads to injustice. Phinehas was both zealous AND
   wise — he confronted AND listened. We need both.

3. Concern for future generations (vv.24-27). The eastern tribes built the
   altar thinking about their children and grandchildren. They feared their
   descendants would be excluded from God's people. Do we think
   generationally? What are we building today that will testify to our
   children that we belong to the LORD?

4. Corporate responsibility (vv.17-20). The delegation understood that one
   group's sin affects the whole community. Peor and Achan proved this. We
   are not isolated individuals — our faithfulness or unfaithfulness
   impacts the body. Sin is never merely "personal."

5. The danger of distance (v.25). The Jordan River created a physical
   barrier that the eastern tribes feared would become a spiritual barrier.
   Physical distance from God's people can lead to spiritual distance from
   God. Stay connected to the body of believers.

6. Joshua's parting charge (v.5). Five words summarize covenant life: love,
   walk, keep, cleave, serve. These are not five separate things but one
   life of devotion expressed in five dimensions. Love is the root; the
   others are its fruit.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Joshua Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/joshua/

David Guzik — Joshua 22 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/joshua-22/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

23.notes:
Joshua - Chapter 23
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
An aging Joshua gives a farewell address, warning Israel that God will drive them out if they intermarry with Canaanites and serve their gods. He reminds them that God has faithfully kept every promise.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Joshua addresses Israel at an unspecified location (likely Shiloh or Timnath-serah) near the end of his life
- The territory described: "from the Jordan to the Great Sea" — the full breadth of the promised land
- Nations remaining among Israel: scattered throughout the tribal territories, a persistent threat

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Joshua      | 13      | 1                  | Joshua "old and stricken in years" at the beginning of the distribution.
v.3              | Deuteronomy | 1       | 30                 | "The LORD your God which goes before you, he shall fight for you."
v.5              | Exodus      | 23      | 30                 | "By little and little I will drive them out from before you."
v.6              | Joshua      | 1       | 7                  | God's original charge: "Be you strong and very courageous... turn not from it to the right hand or to the left."
v.7              | Exodus      | 23      | 13                 | "Make no mention of the name of other gods."
v.8              | Deuteronomy | 10      | 20                 | "You shall fear the LORD your God; him shall you serve, and to him shall you cleave."
v.10             | Leviticus   | 26      | 8                  | "Five of you shall chase an hundred, and an hundred of you shall put ten thousand to flight."
v.10             | Deuteronomy | 32      | 30                 | "How should one chase a thousand, and two put ten thousand to flight?"
v.12             | Deuteronomy | 7       | 3-4                | "Neither shall you make marriages with them... for they will turn away your son from following me."
v.13             | Numbers     | 33      | 55                 | "Those which you let remain... shall be pricks in your eyes, and thorns in your sides."
v.13             | Judges      | 2       | 3                  | "They shall be as thorns in your sides, and their gods shall be a snare to you."
v.14             | Joshua      | 21      | 45                 | "There failed not ought of any good thing which the LORD had spoken."
v.14             | 1 Kings     | 8       | 56                 | Solomon echoes Joshua's testimony.
v.16             | Deuteronomy | 28      | 15-68              | The curses for covenant violation.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/joshua-23/
  Guzik notes the balance in Joshua's farewell: past faithfulness (God
  kept every promise) and future warning (He will also keep His threats).
  Joshua does not leave Israel with false comfort. He loves them enough
  to warn them. Guzik also highlights v.11 — "love the LORD your God" —
  as the single most important command. Everything else flows from this.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Joshua says, 'Not one thing hath failed.' This is the testimony of a
  man who walked with God for sixty years through wilderness and war. He
  tested every promise and found them all true. Can you say the same?
  Look back over your life — has God ever failed you? Has one word of
  His fallen to the ground? Then trust Him for the future. What He has
  been, He will be. What He has done, He will do."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Joshua waxed old and stricken in age" / ESV: "Joshua was old and well advanced in years" — Hebrew "zaqen ba bayamim." Same phrase as 13:1. KJV's "waxed old" uses archaic "waxed" (grew/became). "Stricken in age" = advanced in years.
v.6              | KJV         | "Be ye therefore very courageous to keep and to do" / ESV: "be very strong to keep and to do" — Hebrew "chazaqtem m'od lishmor v'la'asot." "Chazaq" = be strong, courageous, firm. Joshua echoes God's words to him (1:7). The courage needed is for obedience, not just battle.
v.7              | KJV         | "neither make mention of the names of their gods, nor cause to swear by them" / ESV: "or make mention of the names of their gods or swear by them" — Hebrew "lo tazkiru" (not mention) and "lo tashbi'u" (not swear). Even speaking pagan gods' names in oaths was forbidden — it acknowledged their existence and power.
v.8              | KJV         | "cleave unto the LORD" / ESV: "cling to the LORD" — Hebrew "davaq" again (as in 22:5). The marriage word. Intimate, permanent attachment. The opposite of "cleaving to" the remaining nations (v.12, same word).
v.10             | KJV         | "One man of you shall chase a thousand" / ESV: "One man of you puts to flight a thousand" — Hebrew "ish echad mikkhem yirdof elef." "Radaph" = to pursue, chase, put to flight. This supernatural ratio demonstrates that Israel's power is not numerical but divine.
v.13             | KJV         | "scourges in your sides" / ESV: "a whip on your sides" — Hebrew "shotet b'tsiddeikhem." "Shotet" = whip, scourge. The remaining nations will not merely coexist — they will actively torment Israel. What was tolerated becomes what punishes.
v.14             | KJV         | "I am going the way of all the earth" / ESV: "I am about to go the way of all the earth" — Hebrew "holekh ha-yom b'derekh kol ha-aretz." A euphemism for death — "the way all the earth goes." Every human walks this path. Joshua faces death with the same courage he brought to battle.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Way of All the Earth | A euphemism for death (cf. 1 Kings 2:2 — David uses the same phrase). Every person, no matter how great, must walk this path. Joshua faces it with faith and uses his final moments to exhort faithfulness. | 
Cleave/Cling (davaq) | To adhere, hold fast, be joined to. Used of marriage (Genesis 2:24) and of devotion to God. Joshua uses it both positively (cleave to God, v.8) and as a warning (do not cleave to the nations, v.12). We will cling to something — the question is what. | 
Snares and Traps  | Hebrew "pach" (snare, bird trap) and "moqesh" (bait, lure, trap). The remaining nations will entice Israel into sin — not through open warfare but through subtle attraction. The danger is seduction, not assault. | 
Scourges/Whips    | Hebrew "shotet." What Israel tolerated would become what tormented them. The nations they failed to drive out would become instruments of divine discipline. | 
Book of the Law of Moses | The written Torah, the Pentateuch. Joshua's authority rests on this written Word. Obedience to it is the condition for continued blessing in the land. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Not one thing hath failed" (v.14). Joshua's deathbed testimony is God's
   perfect faithfulness. After a lifetime of testing God's promises, his
   verdict is: not one failed. Can we say the same? Look back — has God
   ever broken His word to you? Then trust Him for what lies ahead.

2. God's faithfulness cuts both ways (v.15). He is as faithful to judge as
   to bless. His warnings are as certain as His promises. We cannot claim
   His blessings while ignoring His conditions. Grace is not license. Mercy
   does not cancel holiness.

3. "Be very courageous to keep and to do" (v.6). Obedience requires courage
   — especially when surrounded by a culture that mocks God's standards. It
   takes more courage to obey than to fight. The hardest battles are
   internal, not external.

4. "Love the LORD your God" (v.11). This is the root command. All obedience
   flows from love. All apostasy begins with love growing cold. Guard your
   heart's affection for God above all else. When love is right, obedience
   follows naturally.

5. The danger of intermarriage/mixture (v.12). Joshua warns against
   intimate association with those who worship other gods. The application
   is not ethnic but spiritual: "Be ye not unequally yoked together with
   unbelievers" (2 Corinthians 6:14). Intimate partnership with the world
   leads to spiritual compromise.

6. Snares, traps, scourges, thorns (v.13). The sins we tolerate become the
   sins that torment us. What we refuse to drive out becomes what drives us
   out. The Canaanites Israel kept became the instruments of Israel's
   suffering. Deal with sin completely — or it will deal with you.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Joshua Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/joshua/

David Guzik — Joshua 23 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/joshua-23/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles Spurgeon (Public Domain)

24.ESV:
1. Joshua gathered all the tribes of Israel to Shechem and summoned the elders, the heads, the judges, and the officers of Israel. And they presented themselves before God.
2. And Joshua said to all the people, “Thus says the LORD, the God of Israel, ‘Long ago, your fathers lived beyond the Euphrates, Terah, the father of Abraham and of Nahor; and they served other gods.
3. Then I took your father Abraham from beyond the River and led him through all the land of Canaan, and made his offspring many. I gave him Isaac.
4. And to Isaac I gave Jacob and Esau. And I gave Esau the hill country of Seir to possess, but Jacob and his children went down to Egypt.
5. And I sent Moses and Aaron, and I plagued Egypt with what I did in the midst of it, and afterward I brought you out.
6. “‘Then I brought your fathers out of Egypt, and you came to the sea. And the Egyptians pursued your fathers with chariots and horsemen to the Red Sea.
7. And when they cried to the LORD, he put darkness between you and the Egyptians and made the sea come upon them and cover them; and your eyes saw what I did in Egypt. And you lived in the wilderness a long time.
8. Then I brought you to the land of the Amorites, who lived on the other side of the Jordan. They fought with you, and I gave them into your hand, and you took possession of their land, and I destroyed them before you.
9. Then Balak the son of Zippor, king of Moab, arose and fought against Israel. And he sent and invited Balaam the son of Beor to curse you,
10. but I would not listen to Balaam. Indeed, he blessed you. So I delivered you out of his hand.
11. And you went over the Jordan and came to Jericho, and the leaders of Jericho fought against you, and also the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Girgashites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites. And I gave them into your hand.
12. And I sent the hornet before you, which drove them out before you, the two kings of the Amorites; it was not by your sword or by your bow.
13. I gave you a land on which you had not labored and cities that you had not built, and you dwell in them. You eat the fruit of vineyards and olive orchards that you did not plant.’
14. “Now therefore fear the LORD and serve him in sincerity and in faithfulness. Put away the gods that your fathers served beyond the River and in Egypt, and serve the LORD.
15. And if it is evil in your eyes to serve the LORD, choose this day whom you will serve, whether the gods your fathers served in the region beyond the River, or the gods of the Amorites in whose land you dwell. But as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.”
16. Then the people answered, “Far be it from us that we should forsake the LORD to serve other gods,
17. for it is the LORD our God who brought us and our fathers up from the land of Egypt, out of the house of slavery, and who did those great signs in our sight and preserved us in all the way that we went, and among all the peoples through whom we passed.
18. And the LORD drove out before us all the peoples, the Amorites who lived in the land. Therefore we also will serve the LORD, for he is our God.”
19. But Joshua said to the people, “You are not able to serve the LORD, for he is a holy God. He is a jealous God; he will not forgive your transgressions or your sins.
20. If you forsake the LORD and serve foreign gods, then he will turn and do you harm and consume you, after having done you good.”
21. And the people said to Joshua, “No, but we will serve the LORD.”
22. Then Joshua said to the people, “You are witnesses against yourselves that you have chosen the LORD, to serve him.” And they said, “We are witnesses.”
23. He said, “Then put away the foreign gods that are among you, and incline your heart to the LORD, the God of Israel.”
24. And the people said to Joshua, “The LORD our God we will serve, and his voice we will obey.”
25. So Joshua made a covenant with the people that day, and put in place statutes and rules for them at Shechem.
26. And Joshua wrote these words in the Book of the Law of God. And he took a large stone and set it up there under the terebinth that was by the sanctuary of the LORD.
27. And Joshua said to all the people, “Behold, this stone shall be a witness against us, for it has heard all the words of the LORD that he spoke to us. Therefore it shall be a witness against you, lest you deal falsely with your God.”
28. So Joshua sent the people away, every man to his inheritance.
29. After these things Joshua the son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died, being 110 years old.
30. And they buried him in his own inheritance at Timnath-serah, which is in the hill country of Ephraim, north of the mountain of Gaash.
31. Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua, and all the days of the elders who outlived Joshua and had known all the work that the LORD did for Israel.
32. As for the bones of Joseph, which the people of Israel brought up from Egypt, they buried them at Shechem, in the piece of land that Jacob bought from the sons of Hamor the father of Shechem for a hundred pieces of money. It became an inheritance of the descendants of Joseph.
33. And Eleazar the son of Aaron died, and they buried him at Gibeah, the town of Phinehas his son, which had been given him in the hill country of Ephraim.

1. Joshua assembled all the Israelite tribes at Shechem. He summoned Israel’s elders, rulers, judges, and leaders, and they appeared before God.
2. Joshua told all the people, “This is what the Lord God of Israel has said: ‘In the distant past your ancestors lived beyond the Euphrates River, including Terah the father of Abraham and Nahor. They worshiped other gods,
3. but I took your father Abraham from beyond the Euphrates and brought him into the entire land of Canaan. I made his descendants numerous; I gave him Isaac,
4. and to Isaac I gave Jacob and Esau. To Esau I assigned Mount Seir, while Jacob and his sons went down to Egypt.
5. I sent Moses and Aaron, and I struck Egypt down when I intervened in their land. Then I brought you out.
6. When I brought your fathers out of Egypt, you arrived at the sea. The Egyptians chased your fathers with chariots and horsemen to the Red Sea.
7. Your fathers cried out for help to the Lord; he made the area between you and the Egyptians dark, and then he drowned them in the sea. You witnessed with your very own eyes what I did in Egypt. You lived in the wilderness for a long time.
8. Then I brought you to the land of the Amorites who lived east of the Jordan. They fought with you, but I handed them over to you; you conquered their land, and I destroyed them from before you.
9. Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab, launched an attack against Israel. He summoned Balaam son of Beor to call down judgment on you.
10. I refused to respond to Balaam; he kept prophesying good things about you, and I rescued you from his power.
11. You crossed the Jordan and came to Jericho. The leaders of Jericho, as well as the Amorites, Perizzites, Canaanites, Hittites, Girgashites, Hivites, and Jebusites, fought against you, but I handed them over to you.
12. I sent terror ahead of you to drive out before you the two Amorite kings. I gave you the victory; it was not by your swords or bows.
13. I gave you a land in which you had not worked hard; you took up residence in cities you did not build, and you are eating the produce of vineyards and olive groves you did not plant.’
14. “Now obey the Lord and worship him with integrity and loyalty. Put aside the gods your ancestors worshiped beyond the Euphrates and in Egypt, and worship the Lord.
15. If you have no desire to worship the Lord, then choose today whom you will worship, whether it be the gods whom your ancestors worshiped beyond the Euphrates or the gods of the Amorites in whose land you are living. But I and my family will worship the Lord.”
16. The people responded, “Far be it from us to abandon the Lord so we can worship other gods!
17. For the Lord our God took us and our fathers out of slavery in the land of Egypt and performed these awesome miracles before our very eyes. He continually protected us as we traveled and when we passed through nations.
18. The Lord drove out from before us all the nations, including the Amorites who lived in the land. So we too will worship the Lord, for he is our God!”
19. Joshua warned the people, “You will not keep worshiping the Lord, for he is a holy God. He is a jealous God who will not forgive your rebellion or your sins.
20. If you abandon the Lord and worship foreign gods, he will turn against you; he will bring disaster on you and destroy you, though he once treated you well.”

23. Joshua said, “Now put aside the foreign gods that are among you and submit to the Lord God of Israel.”
24. The people said to Joshua, “We will worship the Lord our God and obey him.”

26. Joshua wrote these words in the Law Scroll of God. He then took a large stone and set it up there under the oak tree near the Lord’s sanctuary.
27. Joshua said to all the people, “Look, this stone will be a witness against us, for it has heard everything the Lord said to us. It will be a witness against you if you deny your God.”

24.notes:
Joshua - Chapter 24
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Joshua gathers all Israel at Shechem, recounts God's faithfulness from Abraham to the conquest, and calls them to choose whom they will serve. The people covenant to serve the LORD, and Joshua dies at 110 years old.

MAP & GEOGRAPHY NOTES
------------------------------------
- Shechem: Located between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim in the central highlands — Israel's covenant renewal site
- "Beyond the River" (v.2): Abraham's origin in Ur/Haran, beyond the Euphrates River in Mesopotamia
- Joshua buried at Timnath-serah in the hill country of Ephraim; Joseph's bones buried at Shechem

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Genesis     | 12      | 6-7                | God's first promise to Abraham at Shechem.
v.1              | Genesis     | 35      | 4                  | Jacob buries foreign gods under the oak at Shechem.
v.1              | Joshua      | 8       | 30-35              | Covenant ceremony at Ebal/Gerizim near Shechem.
v.2              | Genesis     | 11      | 31                 | Terah takes Abraham from Ur of the Chaldees.
v.2              | Isaiah      | 51      | 1-2                | "Look to the rock where you are hewn."
v.3              | Genesis     | 12      | 1-4                | God calls Abraham.
v.5              | Exodus      | 3       | 10                 | God sends Moses.
v.6              | Exodus      | 14      | 21-28              | The Red Sea crossing.
v.9              |             |         |                    | Numbers 22-24 — Balak and Balaam.
v.12             | Exodus      | 23      | 28                 | "I will send hornets before you."
v.13             | Deuteronomy | 6       | 10-11              | "Houses full of all good things, which you filled not."
v.14             | Deuteronomy | 10      | 12                 | "What does the LORD your God require of you, but to fear the LORD your God, to serve him."
v.15             | 1 Kings     | 18      | 21                 | Elijah: "How long halt you between two opinions?"
v.15             | Matthew     | 6       | 24                 | "No man can serve two masters."
v.19             | Exodus      | 20      | 5                  | "I the LORD your God am a jealous God."
v.23             | Genesis     | 35      | 2                  | Jacob: "Put away the strange gods that are among you."
v.25             | Exodus      | 24      | 3-8                | Moses' covenant ceremony at Sinai.
v.29             | Judges      | 2       | 8                  | "Joshua the son of Nun, the servant of the LORD, died."
v.31             | Judges      | 2       | 7                  | ,10 — "Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua... there arose another generation... which knew not the LORD."
v.32             | Genesis     | 50      | 25                 | Joseph's oath: "God will surely visit you, and you shall carry up my bones from hence."
v.32             | Exodus      | 13      | 19                 | Moses takes Joseph's bones from Egypt.
v.32             | Genesis     | 33      | 19                 | Jacob buys the parcel of ground at Shechem.
v.32             | Hebrews     | 11      | 22                 | "By faith Joseph... gave commandment concerning his bones."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/joshua-24/
  Guzik emphasizes that Joshua's "choose you this day" is not an
  invitation to consider options but a demand for decisive commitment.
  The choice is not whether to serve — everyone serves something — but
  WHOM to serve. He also notes Joshua's surprising "Ye cannot serve the
  LORD" (v.19) as a test of sincerity: Joshua wants them to count the
  cost before pledging. Cheap commitment is worthless to a holy God.

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'Choose you this day whom ye will serve.' Not tomorrow — today. Not
  eventually — now. Joshua does not say, 'Think about it.' He says,
  'Choose.' And then he leads: 'As for me and my house, we will serve
  the LORD.' He does not wait for a committee vote. He does not take a
  poll. He declares his allegiance and invites others to follow. This is
  leadership. This is faith. This is the courage of conviction that
  changes nations."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.2              | KJV         | "on the other side of the flood" / ESV: "beyond the Euphrates" — Hebrew "b'ever ha-nahar." "Nahar" = river (the Euphrates). KJV's "flood" is archaic English for "river" (not Noah's flood). ESV correctly identifies it as the Euphrates. Abraham's family lived in Mesopotamia (Ur, then Haran) — beyond the great river.
v.2              | KJV         | "they served other gods" / ESV: "they served other gods" — A startling admission: Abraham's own family were idolaters. Jewish tradition (based on this verse) says Terah made and sold idols. God's choice of Abraham was pure grace — not a reward for prior faithfulness.
v.12             | KJV         | "I sent the hornet before you" / ESV: "I sent the hornet before you" — Hebrew "ha-tsir'ah." "Tsir'ah" = hornet, wasp. Debated whether this is literal (actual hornets/wasps) or figurative (terror, panic, or Egypt — whose symbol was a bee/ hornet). Either way, God claims the victory: "not with thy sword, nor with thy bow."
v.14             | KJV         | "in sincerity and in truth" / ESV: "in sincerity and in faithfulness" — Hebrew "b'tamim u-v'emet." "Tamim" = complete, whole, without blemish (same word used of sacrificial animals). "Emet" = truth, faithfulness, reliability. KJV's "truth" and ESV's "faithfulness" both translate "emet" — which encompasses both concepts.
v.15             | KJV         | "choose you this day" / ESV: "choose this day" — Hebrew "bacharu lakhem ha-yom." "Bachar" = to choose, select, prefer. The imperative is plural — addressed to all Israel. The choice is individual but made publicly, corporately, decisively.
v.19             | KJV         | "Ye cannot serve the LORD" / ESV: "You are not able to serve the LORD" — Hebrew "lo tukh'lu la'avod et YHWH." "Yakol" = to be able. Joshua's statement is provocative — designed to test their resolve. He is saying: "Do you understand what you're committing to? This God is holy and jealous. Half-hearted service is worse than none."
v.26             | KJV         | "under an oak" / ESV: "under the terebinth" — Hebrew "tachat ha-elah." "Elah" = terebinth (a large tree, not technically an oak). KJV's "oak" is a common but imprecise translation. This may be the same tree under which Jacob buried the foreign gods (Genesis 35:4) — a fitting location for covenant renewal.
v.29             | KJV         | "the servant of the LORD" / ESV: "the servant of the LORD" — Hebrew "eved YHWH." Joshua's final title — the same given to Moses (Deuteronomy 34:5). In chapter 1:1, Joshua was "Moses' minister/assistant." Now he is "the servant of the LORD" — he has grown into his own direct relationship with God.
v.32             | KJV         | "for an hundred pieces of silver" / ESV: "for a hundred pieces of money" — Hebrew "b'me'ah q'sitah." "Qesitah" is an ancient unit of value — its exact worth is unknown. KJV's "silver" is interpretive; ESV's "money" is more cautious. The term appears only here, Genesis 33:19, and Job 42:11.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Shechem           | A city in the central hill country between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim. One of the most historically significant sites in Israel: Abraham's first altar (Genesis 12:6-7), Jacob's well and land purchase (Genesis 33:18-19), the covenant renewal here, and later the first capital of the northern kingdom (1 Kings 12:25). | 
Choose (bachar)   | To select, prefer, decide for. In Hebrew thought, choosing is not merely intellectual preference but decisive commitment expressed in action. To "choose" God means to serve Him exclusively. | 
Sincerity (tamim) | Completeness, wholeness, integrity. The same word used for unblemished sacrificial animals. Service to God must be whole — not partial, not divided, not defective. | 
Jealous God (El qanna) | God's jealousy is not petty envy but the righteous demand of exclusive devotion from those in covenant with Him. As a husband rightly demands his wife's exclusive love, God demands His people's exclusive worship. | 
Servant of the LORD (eved YHWH) | The highest title given to God's chosen leaders: Moses (Deuteronomy 34:5), Joshua (24:29), David (Psalm 36 title), and ultimately the Messiah (Isaiah 42:1; 52:13). It denotes both humble submission and honored commission. | 
Joseph's Bones    | Joseph made Israel swear to carry his bones back to Canaan (Genesis 50:25). Moses took them from Egypt (Exodus 13:19). Now, after the conquest, they are finally buried at Shechem — in the land Jacob purchased (Genesis 33:19). A 400-year promise fulfilled. | 
Terebinth (elah)  | A large, long-lived tree common in Canaan. Often associated with sacred sites. The terebinth at Shechem was likely ancient and well-known — a landmark for covenant ceremonies. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Choose you this day whom ye will serve" (v.15). The most urgent
   question any person faces. There is no neutrality — everyone serves
   something. The only question is WHAT. Joshua presents the options
   clearly: dead gods of the past, local gods of the culture, or the living
   LORD. Choose. Today. Not tomorrow.

2. "As for me and my house, we will serve the LORD" (v.15). Joshua does not
   wait for consensus. He leads by declaring his own commitment first.
   Leaders go first. Fathers go first. The household follows the head. What
   have you declared for your house?

3. Grace precedes demand (vv.2-13). Before God asks for commitment, He
   recounts His gifts. "I took... I gave... I sent... I brought... I
   delivered." The call to serve is grounded in what God has already done.
   We do not serve to earn His favor — we serve because we have already
   received it.

4. "Ye cannot serve the LORD" (v.19). Joshua's shocking statement tests
   their sincerity. Serving God is not casual. He is holy, jealous, and
   will not tolerate rivals. Count the cost before you pledge (Luke 14:28).
   Better to not vow than to vow and not pay (Ecclesiastes 5:5).

5. "Put away the strange gods which are among you" (v.23). Even after the
   conquest, some Israelites still had foreign gods! Commitment to the LORD
   requires active removal of rivals. What "gods" do we still harbor —
   comfort, approval, control, pleasure? Put them away. Today.

6. Joseph's bones buried at Shechem (v.32). A 400-year-old promise
   fulfilled. Joseph believed God would bring Israel to Canaan — and made
   them swear to bring his bones. Faith looks beyond death. Faith makes
   plans for a future it will not see. Joseph's bones testify: God keeps
   His word across centuries.

7. "Israel served the LORD all the days of Joshua" (v.31). One generation's
   faithfulness. But the next generation "knew not the LORD" (Judges 2:10).
   Faithfulness is not automatically inherited. Each generation must choose
   for itself. We cannot coast on our parents' faith.

8. Three burials close the book (vv.29-33). Joshua, Joseph, Eleazar — the
   leader, the patriarch, the priest. An era ends. The generation that
   conquered Canaan passes away. What remains? The land, the covenant, the
   choice — and the question: will the next generation serve the LORD?
